behaviors.description: Log entries with the same trace ID are grouped together regardless of which microservice emitted them
behaviors.justification: A unit test of the grouping logic alone cannot catch bugs in JSON parsing, trace ID extraction across different log schemas, or coordination of multi-service sources. Real failures happen at the pipeline boundary: extracting trace IDs from heterogeneous JSON structures and correctly correlating them. Must parse actual JSON from multiple simulated services.
behaviors.test_description: Ingest valid JSON logs from three simulated microservices (API, worker, database) where multiple log entries share the same trace ID but differ in source, timestamp, and event type. Verify: (1) all entries with identical trace ID are grouped in the same correlation group, (2) no entries are lost or duplicated during grouping, (3) entries from different services are correctly attributed to their source.

behaviors.description: The timeline view orders entries by timestamp, correctly handling clock skew of up to 500ms between services
behaviors.justification: The ordering algorithm itself (Unit) could be tested with mock data, but real failures occur when handling heterogeneous timestamps from multiple services with realistic clock drift. The real boundary is parsing timestamps from actual log entries, coordinating them across services, and verifying the clock-skew correction logic works end-to-end. Must ingest logs from multiple sources with simulated clock offsets.
behaviors.test_description: Ingest logs from multiple services where service A's clock is 500ms ahead of service B, and service C is 300ms behind baseline. Verify: (1) timeline correctly reorders entries despite the 500ms max skew, (2) no dropped or out-of-order entries in the final timeline, (3) clock skew metadata is recorded or corrected appropriately, (4) timestamp parsing does not fail on different ISO 8601 formats from different services.

behaviors.description: A latency spike (p99 above 3x the baseline) is detected and flagged in the anomaly report
behaviors.justification: The p99 calculation or spike detection algorithm alone (Unit) could be tested, but real failures occur in the full pipeline: latency extraction from logs, aggregation logic, baseline establishment, and spike flagging. A unit test with mock latencies misses bugs in log parsing, missing latency fields, or off-by-one errors in windowing. Must ingest actual log entries with measured latencies.
behaviors.test_description: Ingest logs with measured latency values. Establish a baseline of 100ms p99 (requests with latencies 10–150ms). Then inject a spike scenario (50% of requests exceed 300ms, pushing p99 to 320ms = 3.2x baseline). Verify: (1) the spike is detected and flagged in the anomaly report, (2) the baseline is correctly calculated from the initial window, (3) no false positives when latencies stay within bounds, (4) spike detection threshold is exactly 3x baseline.

behaviors.description: Malformed JSON log entries are skipped without crashing the pipeline, and the count of skipped entries is reported
behaviors.justification: Exception handling and error counting could be unit-tested in isolation, but real failures occur when the pipeline encounters malformed JSON in a continuous stream. A unit test with a single try-catch misses: incomplete log entries, truncated JSON, mixed encodings, and subtle malformation patterns that only appear under realistic load. Must stream realistic malformed data through the full pipeline.
behaviors.test_description: Stream a sequence of 1000 log entries mixing valid JSON (800 entries) with malformed entries: syntax errors (missing quotes), truncated JSON (incomplete objects), missing required fields, invalid UTF-8, and excessively nested structures. Verify: (1) pipeline does not crash, (2) all 800 valid entries are processed correctly, (3) skipped count equals 200, (4) no valid entries are incorrectly skipped, (5) error log entries are created for each malformed entry.

behaviors.description: The tool handles a burst of 10,000 log entries per second without dropping entries or falling behind
behaviors.justification: Throughput and queueing logic cannot be tested at Unit or Integration level with real timing guarantees. Real failures happen under OS-level I/O and scheduling constraints: buffer overflow, thread starvation, file handle exhaustion, or kernel queue limits. A pure in-memory integration test with mock timing hides real bottlenecks in file I/O, network handling, or subprocess behavior. Must generate and measure real throughput under actual I/O and timing conditions.
behaviors.test_description: Generate 10,000 valid log entries per second for 10 seconds (100,000 total). Feed them through the tool via the production ingestion path (file, network socket, or message queue). Measure: (1) zero dropped entries (verify all 100,000 are processed), (2) no backlog accumulation (latency to process each batch remains stable), (3) end-to-end latency per entry does not exceed 5 seconds even at peak throughput, (4) resource usage (file descriptors, memory, threads) does not exceed acceptable limits.
